text_statement: Scientists are investigating the variation in air pressure at different locations on a mountain. The scientists take measurements... Complete Table 1.1 by stating the SI base unit for each quantity and identifying whether each quantity is a scalar or a vector.

text_statement: At one location, the density of the air is 1.1 kg m^-3. A spherical weather balloon is filled with a gas and released from rest. The balloon has radius 0.90 m. Calculate the upthrust acting on the balloon when it is released.

text_statement: Explain why an upthrust acts on the balloon.

text_statement: A quantity c relating to the motion of the balloon is calculated from three measured quantities k, F and v using the formula c = (2kF)/v^2. label: a(i)
text_statement:
A spacecraft in deep space uses jets of hot gas from its thrusters to change its velocity.
Thruster A is a distance of 1.6 m leftwards from the centre of gravity. Thruster C is a distance of 0.40 m upwards. Thruster A produces a force of 60 N upwards. Thruster C produces a force of 220 N leftwards. Calculate the resultant moment due to these forces about the centre of gravity.

label: a(ii)
text_statement: State and explain whether the forces from A and C are a couple.

text_statement:

text_statement: Show that the mass of the payload is 230 kg.

label: c(iii)
text_statement: Calculate the magnitude of the change in velocity of the carrier.

text_statement:

text_statement:
hour 15 minutes
You must answer on the multiple choice answer sheet.
You will need: Multiple choice answer sheet
Soft clean eraser
Soft pencil (type B or HB is recommended)
INSTRUCTIONS

There are forty questions on this paper. Answer all questions.

For each question there are four possible answers A, B, C and D. Choose the one you consider correct
and record your choice in soft pencil on the multiple choice answer sheet.

Follow the instructions on the multiple choice answer sheet.

Write in soft pencil.

Write your name, centre number and candidate number on the multiple choice answer sheet in the
spaces provided unless this has been done for you.

Do not use correction fluid.

Do not write on any bar codes.


text_statement:
An ice cube of mass 37.0 g at temperature 0.0 °C is placed in a beaker containing water of mass 208 g at temperature 26.4 °C.
When all the ice has melted, and all the water in the beaker has reached thermal equilibrium, the final temperature of all the water is 10.3 °C.
The specific heat capacity of water is 4.18 J g–1 °C–1.
The beaker has negligible specific heat capacity and is perfectly insulated from the surroundings.
Determine a value, to three significant figures, for the specific latent heat of fusion of water.

label: a(i)
text_statement: State what is meant by the internal energy of a system.

label: a(ii)
text_statement: Explain why the internal energy of an ideal gas is directly proportional to the thermodynamic temperature of the gas.

label: b
text_statement:
A sample of an ideal gas at thermodynamic temperature T has internal energy U.
The gas is compressed so that its temperature increases to 3T.
During this compression, work W is done on the gas.
The gas is then cooled at constant volume so that its temperature decreases to 2T.
Complete Table 4.1 to show, in terms of some or all of W, T and U, the work done on the gas, the thermal energy supplied to the gas and the increase in internal energy of the gas for each of the two processes.

label: a(i)
text_statement:
A cuboidal block floats in a liquid with its base horizontal, as shown in Fig. 5.1.
[diagram]
The base of the block is at a depth h below the surface of the liquid.
The block is displaced downwards by a small distance and then released so that it oscillates.
Fig. 5.2 shows the variation with h of the acceleration a of the block.
[diagram]
Fig. 5.3 shows the variation with h of the kinetic energy EK of the block.
[diagram]
Determine the amplitude of the oscillations.

label: a(ii)
text_statement: State what the line in Fig. 5.2 shows about the nature of the oscillations.

label: b
text_statement: State three other quantitative conclusions that can be drawn from Fig. 5.2 and Fig. 5.3 about the block and its oscillations.

label: c
text_statement:
On Fig. 5.4, sketch the variation with h of the potential energy EP of the oscillations.
[diagram]

label: a(i)
text_statement:
Fig. 6.1 shows a circuit that rectifies an alternating input voltage VIN and produces an output voltage VOUT across a resistor R.
[diagram]
The four terminals of the rectification circuit are labelled W, X, Y and Z.
A capacitor C is connected in parallel with resistor R.
State what is meant by rectification.

label: a(ii)
text_statement: State the purpose of capacitor C.

label: b(i)
text_statement:
Fig. 6.2 shows the variations with time t of the potential differences (p.d.s) VIN and VOUT.
[diagram]
The variation of VIN with t can be represented by
VIN = A cos Bt
where A and B are constants.
Determine the values of A and B.

label: b(ii)
text_statement: Determine the type of rectification produced by the circuit in Fig. 6.1.

label: b(iii)
text_statement:
On Fig. 6.3, draw the circuit diagram for the components inside the rectification circuit.
[diagram]

label: b(iv)
text_statement: Determine a value for the time constant for the discharge of the capacitor C through the resistor R in Fig. 6.1.

label: c
text_statement:
The capacitor C has a capacitance of 570 μF.
Use your answer in (b)(iv) to determine the resistance of resistor R.

label: a
text_statement: Define magnetic flux density.

label: b(i)
text_statement:
A particle of mass m and charge +Q moves at speed v into a region where there is a uniform magnetic field, as shown in Fig. 7.1.
[diagram]
The uniform magnetic field is into the page and has flux density B. The particle enters the region of the field at point Y.
State an expression, in terms of some or all of m, Q, B and v, for the magnetic force F that acts on the particle when it is at point Y.

label: b(ii)
text_statement: On Fig. 7.1, draw an arrow at point Y to indicate the direction of the force in (b)(i).

label: b(iii)
text_statement: On Fig. 7.1, draw a line to show a possible path for the particle through the region of the magnetic field.

label: c(i)
text_statement: Explain how an electric field can be used with the magnetic field to ensure that the particle in (b) now passes through point Z.

label: c(ii)
text_statement: Derive an expression for v in terms of B and the electric field strength E.

label: a
text_statement: State what is meant by the de Broglie wavelength.

label: b
text_statement: Calculate the de Broglie wavelength of an electron moving at a speed of 4.9 × 10^7 m s–1.

label: c
text_statement: State one similarity and one difference between an electron and a positron.

label: d(i)
text_statement:
An electron moving at a speed of 4.9 × 10^7 m s–1 collides with a positron that is travelling at the same speed in the opposite direction. As a result of the collision, two gamma-ray photons are produced.
State the name of the process that occurs when the electron and the positron collide.

label: d(ii)
text_statement: Explain why gamma-ray photons are produced.

label: d(iii)
text_statement: Explain why two gamma-ray photons are produced rather than one.

label: d(iv)
text_statement: Calculate the kinetic energy of the electron.

label: d(v)
text_statement: Calculate the wavelength of each of the two gamma-ray photons.

label: a
text_statement: State what is meant by the activity of a radioactive sample.

label: b
text_statement: Explain why the activity of a radioactive sample containing one isotope varies exponentially with time.

label: c(i)
text_statement:
A radioactive sample initially has an activity of 180 Bq. After a time of 8.4 minutes, the activity of the sample is 120 Bq.
Calculate the decay constant of the isotope in the sample.

label: c(ii)
text_statement: Calculate the half-life of the isotope.

label: c(iii)
text_statement:
On Fig. 9.1, sketch the variation with time t of the activity A of the sample for values of t between t = 0 and t = 24 minutes.
[diagram]

label: a
text_statement: State Hubble’s law.

label: b(i)
text_statement:
A line in the spectrum of light from a laboratory source has a wavelength of 4.62 × 10^–7 m. The same line in the spectrum of light from a distant galaxy is observed to have a wavelength of 4.91 × 10^–7 m.
Explain what can be concluded about the galaxy from the change in wavelength.

label: b(ii)
text_statement: Determine the speed of the galaxy relative to the Earth.

label: b(iii)
text_statement:
A star in the galaxy is observed to have a peak intensity of emitted radiation at a wavelength that is greater than the corresponding wavelength for the Sun.
State the property of the star that can be deduced from this observation.
